question: What is the main requirement for a schedule to be considered recoverable?
options: A transaction commits only after all transactions from which it has read have committed.
All transactions in the schedule are serializable.
No two transactions are allowed to write to the same data item.
Every read operation must be preceded by a write operation.
explanation: A recoverable schedule prevents dirty reads and ensures that a transaction only commits after the transactions that wrote the data it read have successfully committed, avoiding cascading rollbacks.

question: Which of the following schedules is NOT recoverable?
options: T1: R(A), W(A), Commit; T2: R(A), W(A), Commit;
T1: R(A), W(A); T2: R(A), Commit; T1: Abort;
T1: R(A), W(A), Commit; T2: R(A), Commit;
T1: W(A), Commit; T2: R(A), Commit;
explanation: This schedule is not recoverable because T2 committed after reading a value written by T1, but T1 subsequently aborted. T2's commit is now based on uncommitted (and ultimately rolled back) data, which is lost.

question: What is the key property that distinguishes a Cascadeless Schedule (Avoids Cascading Rollbacks) from a merely Recoverable one?
options: Transactions can only read committed data values.
Transactions are not allowed to write to the same data item concurrently.
A transaction must hold exclusive locks until it commits.
The schedule must be conflict serializable.
explanation: A Cascadeless Schedule prevents cascading rollbacks by ensuring that no transaction can ever read uncommitted data from another transaction. This is a stricter condition than basic recoverability.

question: Consider the following schedule S: T1: W(A); T2: W(A); T1: Commit; T2: Commit;. How would this schedule be characterized?
options: It is Recoverable and Cascadeless.
It is Recoverable but not Cascadeless.
It is not Recoverable.
It is Recoverable, but its classification for cascading rollbacks is indeterminate.
explanation: The schedule is Recoverable because T2's write does not depend on reading any data from T1, so the commit order is irrelevant to recoverability. It is also Cascadeless because there are no read operations at all, so no risk of reading uncommitted data.

question: A schedule is Strict. Which of the following is a necessary and sufficient condition it must satisfy?
options: It must be both Serializable and Recoverable.
A transaction can neither read nor write a data item until the last transaction that wrote it has committed or aborted.
A transaction must obtain a shared lock for reading and an exclusive lock for writing.
The precedence graph of the schedule must be acyclic.
explanation: A Strict schedule is the most restrictive class for recoverability. It requires that a transaction cannot read or write a data item until the last transaction that wrote it (the one holding the "exclusive" right to it) has ended (committed or aborted). This ensures easy recovery by allowing the redo and undo of operations without complicating live transactions.
